design: cross-sectional matching across four acoustic continua and four basic tastes at two concentrations
result: Participants shared systematic preferences linking acoustic properties with tastes and concentrations
limitation: Preference matching is not an induced taste and does not test learned transfer

design: explicit pitch and instrument matching to tastes and flavors
population: adult nonsynesthetes; exact N not stated in PubMed abstract
result: Sweet and sour were preferentially matched with high pitch; umami and bitter with low pitch
limitation: Explicit matching; familiarity and hedonic value influenced instrument choices
relevance: Supports correspondence but not auditory generation of taste

design: participants tasted identical chocolate under creamy and rough soundtracks
population: exact N not stated in accessible PubMed record
result: Music changed reported creaminess and sweetness/bitterness of chocolate
limitation: Chocolate was physically present; not evidence of taste from sound alone

design: fMRI study of music-evoked autobiographical memories
population: healthy participants; full-text N not used here
result: Familiar music-evoked autobiographical memory covaried with medial prefrontal activity
limitation: Familiar music and autobiographical cues differ from novel procedural signals
relevance: Supports a music-memory route without proving novel-cue retrieval

citation: Kershaw; Running (2018), Archives of Oral Biology 92:90-95
design: classical conditioning with a visual cue predicting sour candy
population: human participants; exact N not stated in accessible abstract
result: Saliva weight increased for the candy-paired image and across the first visit
limitation: Salivation is not taste; visual cue and candy do not establish auditory transfer

citation: Avery; Carrington; Martin (2023), Progress in Neurobiology 223:102423
design: high-field fMRI with imagined tastes and food-picture inference
population: human participants; exact N not quoted without full methods
result: Quality-specific patterns for imagined and inferred tastes overlapped in dorsal mid-insula
limitation: Neural overlap does not establish subjective equivalence or auditory causation

citation: Small et al. (2004), Journal of Neurophysiology
design: event-related fMRI comparing congruent and incongruent taste-odor mixtures with unimodal constituents
population: healthy adults; exact N not used from abstract
result: Congruent flavor produced superadditive responses in insula/frontal operculum/OFC and other regions
limitation: Flavor constituents were physically delivered; regional activation cannot be inferred by UMBRAL

design: simultaneous EEG/MEG hierarchical auditory novelty and omission paradigm
result: Unexpected mismatches and omitted expected tones supported at least two levels of auditory prediction
limitation: Neural omission responses require instrumentation and do not prove subjective reconstruction
relevance: Supports omission as a control and prediction probe

citation: Loebach; Pisoni; Svirsky (2008), Journal of the Acoustical Society of America
design: pre/post training with spectrally degraded speech or environmental sounds
population: five training groups of normal-hearing adults; exact group N not quoted from abstract
result: Training improved identification but generalization depended on task and materials
limitation: Degraded speech learning is not crossmodal emotional conditioning
relevance: Supports learning and the need to test transfer rather than assume it

citation: Kucinkas et al. (2025), npj Science of Learning 11(1):4
design: random assignment to traditional reversed or arbitrary image-to-sound mappings
result: Structured mappings were learned within 30 minutes and generalized to novel stimuli; reversed and traditional mappings performed similarly
limitation: Visual-to-auditory substitution is not evidence of sound-induced qualia
relevance: Supports flexible learned mapping and explicit transfer tests

design: critical review of human second-order conditioning evidence
result: The review concludes that human second-order conditioning evidence is comparatively scant
limitation: Review rather than a new experiment; paradigms vary
relevance: Requires UMBRAL to treat second-order transfer as uncertain

design: EEG comparison of binaural and monaural 40-Hz beat responses
population: human listeners; exact N not quoted from abstract
result: A low-carrier 40-Hz binaural-beat ASSR was measurable but smaller than the acoustic-beat response
limitation: ASSR is an EEG measure; browser behavior cannot establish entrainment
relevance: Defines a measurable effect and instrumentation boundary

citation: Wahbeh et al. (2007), Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine 13(2):199-206
design: randomized blinded placebo-controlled crossover pilot of 7-Hz binaural beats
result: No support for scalp-recorded steady-state entrainment; some adverse-direction secondary differences were reported
limitation: Extremely small pilot and many outcomes
relevance: Argues against efficacy assumptions and for adverse-event monitoring

design: single-blind active-controlled comparison of binaural and monaural beats across auditory pathway measures
population: healthy human adults; exact N not quoted here
result: Both beats elicited auditory responses; binaural beats weakly entrained cortex and did not modulate mood
limitation: Neural and connectivity measures do not imply therapeutic or perceptual benefit
relevance: Supports conservative beat implementation with active controls

citation: Orozco Perez et al. (2023), PLOS ONE 18:e0286023
result: Findings were inconsistent: supportive contradictory and mixed results

citation: World Health Organization and ITU (2019), Safe listening devices and systems
result: Recommends exposure-aware listening; adult reference 80 dB for 40 hours/week and volume-limiting information
limitation: UMBRAL cannot measure SPL or cumulative dose from a browser
relevance: Supports low-gain design warnings breaks and device-level volume guidance
